coredata.dc:description: Multi-color phosphorescent materials have received considerable attention owing to their wide range of applications. However, a standardized strategy for producing tunable phosphorescent colors has rarely been developed. Herein, we report the preparation of ingeniously designed silicon-nanodot-based nanocomposite materials with multi-color afterglows (cyan, yellow, orange and red) using a simple hydrothermal method that involves combining silane, dyes and urea. Notably, the silane formed silicon nanodots in situ by participating in the hot-melt recrystallization process of urea, accompanied by the formation of new covalent and hydrogen bonds. These nanodots become immobilized in the rigid network structure formed during urea recrystallization to deliver highly efficient phosphorescence. More impressively, orange and red afterglow materials were successfully synthesized based on the phosphorescence Förster resonance energy transfer principle. These properties led to the establishment of the information security systems using single-, multiple-, and 5D information encryption methods. Given the universality of this method, this standardized strategy not only highlights the potential of constructing multifunctional phosphorescent materials from silane, but also provides a novel design principle for the synthesis of full-color afterglow materials.

(g) Images of four SiNDs@UA powders under 365 nm UV lamp on and off with time.

(c) The internal structure of R-SiNDs@UA inferred from P-FRET principle. Schematic illustration for the preparations of C-SiNDs@UA, Y-SiNDs@UA, O-SiNDs@UA, and R-SiNDs@UA. And a possible mechanism of the photophysical processes of Y-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA composites.

head.ce:title: Multi-Color Room temperature phosphorescent Silicon-Nanodot-Based nanocomposites with silane tuning and applications to 5D information encryption

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparations of C-SiNDs@UA, Y-SiNDs@UA, O-SiNDs@UA, and R-SiNDs@UA. And a possible mechanism of the photophysical processes of C-SiNDs@UA, Y-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA nanocomposites.

Multi-color phosphorescent materials have received considerable attention owing to their wide range of applications. However, a standardized strategy for producing tunable phosphorescent colors has rarely been developed. Herein, we report the preparation of ingeniously designed silicon-nanodot-based nanocomposite materials with multi-color afterglows (cyan, yellow, orange and red) using a simple hydrothermal method that involves combining silane, dyes and urea. Notably, the silane formed silicon nanodots in situ by participating in the hot-melt recrystallization process of urea, accompanied by the formation of new covalent and hydrogen bonds. These nanodots become immobilized in the rigid network structure formed during urea recrystallization to deliver highly efficient phosphorescence. More impressively, orange and red afterglow materials were successfully synthesized based on the phosphorescence Förster resonance energy transfer principle. These properties led to the establishment of the information security systems using single-, multiple-, and 5D information encryption methods. Given the universality of this method, this standardized strategy not only highlights the potential of constructing multifunctional phosphorescent materials from silane, but also provides a novel design principle for the synthesis of full-color afterglow materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials were widely used in information security [1,2], displays and lighting [3,4], photocatalysis and bioimaging applications because of their distinctive afterglow characteristics [5–7]. While the development of new RTP materials has also progressed using various techniques, conventional synthesis methods primarily involve inorganic compounds that contain rare-earth ions or complexes containing precious metals to deliver afterglow, which inevitably leads to high biological toxicity and expensive syntheses [8,9]. Some samples need to be cooled with liquid nitrogen to form low-temperature rigid glasses in order to observe afterglow emissions [10]. Moreover, the use of existing polymer-based RTP material systems is limited, with fabrication processes relying on poly(lactic acid), polyurethane, and poly(methyl methacrylate) [11]. The interference due to molecular vibrations and molecular oxygen during the triplet-state quenching process also cannot be ignored in phosphorescent materials prepared to date [12]. Consequently, realizing multi-color long-afterglow materials is difficult when these complex preparation processes and harsh synthesis conditions are used, which seriously limits the use of phosphorescent materials in a variety of scenarios. Therefore, exploring new strategies for producing color-tunable phosphorescent materials with improved performance is an important objective.

The emergence of carbon dots (CDs) has facilitated the use of new approaches for preparing phosphorescent materials owing to the abundance of raw materials and methods for their synthesis [10,13]. Meanwhile, two preconditions must be satisfied to endow a material with excellent phosphorescence properties, namely efficient exciplex intersystem crossing (ISC) from the singlet to the triplet state, and a stable rigid structure that stabilizes the excited triplet states of the exciplexes while also shielding against nonradiative transitions, thereby directing efficient RTP emissions [10]. The first precondition is conventionally addressed by introducing heteroatoms, such as B, F and P, into CDs to improve intersystem crossing [3,14,15]. While the second involves using a two-step method to fix the CDs in a composite matrix (Polyvinyl alcohol, SiO2, zeolite, etc.) to deliver efficient phosphorescence [16–18]. However, the abovementioned methods also have obvious drawbacks. Doping with either boric acid or a halogen requires a synthesis protocol that is overly dependent on the choice of precursor and cannot effectively regulate the multi-color RTP properties of the material. In addition, the two-step synthesis method involves the mutual selection of subject and object and requires a range of reaction conditions, which increases the risk of uncontrollability; consequently, difficulties in ensuring homogeneity in the first production step directly affect the productivity of the final step [19].

Various silicon nanodots (SiNDs) have recently been used in a number of applications [20,21], SiNDs not only exhibit excellent properties that are similar to those of CDs, but also have some specific advantages, including superior quantum yields and optical stabilities, and a lack of toxicity [22–24]. Meanwhile, as Si is one of the most abundant elements in the Earth’s crust, the use of a Si-based reactant also results in lower costs and higher profitability [25]. Silane is typically used to build rigid structures during the synthesis of a phosphorescent material owing to triplet-exciton stabilization afforded by covalent Si-C and Si-O bonds [18,26]. For example, Sun et al. [27] prepared exceptionally stable RTP materials by synthesizing CDs@SiO2 from rice husks, with stability related to the compact 3D network dominated by Si–O bonds. He et al. [28] improved the intersystem crossing process by constructing CD-decorated amorphous nanosilica; the adamantine CDs-SiO2 structure stabilized triplet-state excitons, thereby enhancing phosphorescence intensity. However, the use of rice husks as a silicon source cannot guarantee product homogeneity or reproducibility. Moreover, using the two-step method to construct a core–satellite-like structure with silane requires a highly pure primary product; any compromise in purity may lead to lower yields and a poorly uniform final product.

In this study, we directly used silane as a precursor for the synthesis of Si-based phosphorescent materials, which simplified the preparation procedure to one involving a one-step hydrothermal process. Accordingly, this strategy not only effectively avoids the disadvantages of the two-step method but also more conveniently forms nanodots in situ and immobilizes them in the matrix [29]. Based on these conditions, we selected 3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) and N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO) as Si sources, with urea added as another precursor, to produce optically stable cyan and yellow RTP materials, respectively. Further experiments also revealed the significant roles that covalent and hydrogen bonds between the silicon nanodots and the matrix play in limiting vibrational relaxation and reducing the triplet-exciton non-radiative inactivation rate, thereby demonstrating the stability and excellent RTP properties of the structure. Intriguingly, the phosphorescence properties of the silicon nanodot system and the rigid network structure satisfies the basic conditions for the T1–S1 phosphorescence Förster resonance energy transfer (P-FRET) principle [30]. Accordingly, we added Rhodamine 6G (Rh 6G) and Rhodamine B (Rh B) to effectively adjust the afterglow color and successfully delivered orange and red afterglow as a consequence. Hence, our study demonstrated the enormous potential of silane-based precursors for the preparation of highly stable, reproducible, and mass-producible RTP materials with a wide range of development prospects for advanced information encryption and other innovative applications.
3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO), Rhodamine 6G (Rh 6G), Rhodamine B (Rh B) and Urea were obtained from Titan Scientific (Shanghai, China).
The characterization methods were presented in the Supplementary Material.

1 g of urea was fully dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and 1 mL of APTES was added, shaken, and ultrasonic treated for 5 min to make the raw materials uniformly mixed. Then transfer the solution to a 50 mL transparent beaker and heat it in an oven at 180 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling, the solid obtained would be completely ground to get the powder and then the powder was redispersed in ultrapure water. Subsequently, the dispersion was processed by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was dropped to remove side products. The precipitate was then washed three times with anhydrous ethanol and finally, the precipitate was dried in an oven at 60 ℃ for 5 h to obtain the purified product.

1 g of urea was fully dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and 1 mL of DAMO was added, shaken, and ultrasonic treated for 5 min to make the raw materials uniformly mixed. Then transfer the solution to a 50 mL transparent beaker and heat it in an oven at 180 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling, the solid obtained was purified by the same process as C-SiNDs@UA to obtain the final product.

1 g of urea and 3 mg of Rh 6G were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and 1 mL of APTES was added, then shaken and sonicated for 5 min to mix the ingredients homogeneously. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL clear beaker and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the solid obtained was purified by the same process as C-SiNDs@UA to obtain the final product.

1 g of urea and 3 mg of Rh B were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and 1 mL of DAMO was added, then shaken and sonicated for 5 min to mix the ingredients homogeneously. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL clear beaker and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the solid obtained was purified by the same process as C-SiNDs@UA to obtain the final product.

1 g of urea was fully dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, then transfer the solution to a 50 mL transparent beaker and heat it in an oven at 180 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling, the solid obtained was purified by the same process as C-SiNDs@UA to obtain the final product.

1 g of purified C-SiNDs@UA and 3 mg of Rh 6G were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water. Next, the solution was shaken and sonicated for 5 min to mix the ingredients homogeneously. Then transfer the solution to a 50 mL transparent beaker and heat it in an oven at 180 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling, the solid obtained was purified by the same process as C-SiNDs@UA to obtain the final product.

1 g of purified Y-SiNDs@UA and 3 mg of Rh B were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water. Next, the solution was shaken and sonicated for 5 min to mix the ingredients homogeneously. Then transfer the solution to a 50 mL transparent beaker and heat it in an oven at 180 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling, the solid obtained was purified by the same process as C-SiNDs@UA to obtain the final product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the information encryption molds
ce:section.ce:para: The butterfly panel was purchased online (Shanghai, China) and was divided into different parts according to the designs, then the four colors of afterglow materials were applied to the respective areas. The mold based on the Moss code was made by 3D printing, after designing the distribution of the color blocks, the afterglow materials were applied to the corresponding areas. In the 5D anti-counterfeiting process, a light filter was added to the mold to capture the filtered fluorescence and afterglow patterns. All patterns were taken in a dark room with a hand-held UV lamp (λ = 365 nm) on or off.

The new silicon-based RTP materials designed in this study were synthesized as shown in Scheme 1 using APTES and DAMO as silicon sources. The crude products were hydrothermally prepared at 180 ℃ for 5 h after being mixed with urea. Then the crude products were purified by redispersing in deionized water and centrifugate procedure to remove side products, and the final RTP materials were obtained by drying process. The prepared materials are referred to as “C-SiNDs@UA” and “Y-SiNDs@UA” according to their phosphorescent colors (C: cyan; Y: yellow), wherein the cyan and yellow phosphorescent materials correspond to APTES and DAMO sources, respectively. We also prepared O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA (O: orange; R: red) by adding Rh 6G to the APTES precursor system, and Rh B to the DAMO precursor system. An undoped phosphorescent material was also synthesized by calcining urea alone and is referred to as “experimental urea” (eUA). The chemical structures of these molecules and polymers are shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Material). Various characterization techniques were used to verify synthesis success and examine the structural properties of the materials.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images displayed in Fig. 1 a-d show that the four materials are composed of almost spherical particles with relatively uniform size distributions ( Fig. S2 ); the C-, Y-, O-, and R-SiNDs@UA particles are 2.55 ± 0.34, 2.38 ± 0.36, 2.53 ± 0.28 and 2.40 ± 0.41 nm in size, respectively. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) revealed that these materials are crystalline, with uniform lattice spacings (0.21 nm) that are related to the (100) facet of graphite, which suggests that the composite contains a graphite-like core [31]. Furthermore, clear SiNDs and matrix structures are observable in the backgrounds of the enlarged TEM images (Fig. S3a-b). These matrix structures are presumed to comprise rigid networks of cyanuric acid (CA) crystals (Fig. S3 d) and triazine ring structures formed by the high-temperature deamination of urea [32,33], as confirmed by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. Fig. S4 a displays the 13C NMR spectrum of pure CA powder, which reveals a characteristic peak at 149.98 ppm. The 13C NMR spectrum of the eUA is shown in Fig. S4 b, in which the peak at 150.07 ppm is characteristic of CA, while the two observed at 155.51 ppm and 159.92 ppm are characteristic C=O and urea peaks [34,35], respectively. The incorporation of silane appears to affect UA crystallinity. We conjecture that silane participates in the hot-melt recrystallization of urea to form silicon nanodots in situ under hydrothermal conditions, accompanied by the formation of new covalent and hydrogen bonds, with the SiNDs immobilized in a rigid network structure consisting of CA crystals and triazine ring structures; this hypothesis was subsequently verified using various characterization techniques. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 1e) show that the abovementioned four SiNDs@UAs exhibit peaks at 2θ values of approximately 21.76°, 21.00°, 21.32° and 20.40°, respectively, which correspond to amorphous silica in these SiNDs [31]. In addition, C-SiNDs@UA and Y-SiNDs@UA show peaks attributable stacked conjugated aromatic systems at 28.04° and 28.18°, respectively [32]. The Raman spectrum of C-SiNDs@UA exhibits peaks at 1342 and 1590 cm−1 that correspond to its D and G bands respectively (Fig. 1f). Similarly, D and G bands were observed at 1333 and 1593 cm−1 in the spectrum of Y-SiNDs@UA, at 1357 and 1596 cm−1 for O-SiNDs@UA, and 1339 and 1622 cm−1 for R-SiNDs@UA. The D bands are related to the breathing modes of the six-membered rings, whereas the G bands are related to the relative motions of sp2 carbon atoms [36].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the four materials (Fig. 1g) and eUA (Fig. S5 a) show that the SiNDs@UAs have very similar characteristic FT-IR peaks; with C-Si peaks observed in the 1239–1278 cm−1 range (not observed in the spectrum of eUA) [6]. In addition, the Si-O peak at 1039–1057 cm−1 further confirms the incorporation of silicon [28]. Furthermore, the CA crystals appear to be stabilized in the solid state in the form of s-triazine-2,4,6 trione, with peaks at 765–769 cm−1 attributed to the triazine ring structure, while the peaks at 1450–1455 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-N=C bond. In addition, the absorption bands at 1713–1719 cm−1 are attributed to C=O stretching vibrations associated with CA; these data confirm that urea had recrystallized in the hot melt state [32,37]. The peaks at 3439–3448 cm−1 are due to N-H/O-H stretching vibration; these bonds are associated with hydrogen bonding [38,39]. Taken together, these results further indicate that the covalent and hydrogen bonding network formed by the SiNDs and the rigid matrix structure, while the matrix was composed of CA crystals and triazine ring structures.

The presence of Si in these phosphorescent materials was further confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), with characteristic Si 2s and Si 2p peaks observed in the spectra of all four materials (Fig. 1h), whereas no silicon-related peaks were observed in the spectrum of eUA, as expected (Fig. S5 b). The full XPS spectrum of C-SiNDs@UA displays peaks corresponding carbon (39.27 %), nitrogen (14.86 %), oxygen (39.65 %) and silicon (6.22 %); these characteristic peaks were observed at 284.7, 399.7, 531.7, 102.7, and 153.7 eV, respectively. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1i) of C-SiNDs@UA was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6, 285.6, 288.7 and 289.2 eV assigned to C-Si, C-N, C=O and N-C=N bonds [40], respectively. While the N 1s XPS spectrum was fitted to three components: N-Si (398.8 eV), N-(C)3 (400.3 eV) and sp2-hybridized nitrogen in triazine rings (C-N=C), which is located at 399.8 eV ( Fig. S6 ) [41]. The O 1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 531.1, 532.0 and 532.7 eV ( Fig. S6 ), which are assigned to C=O, O-Si and C-O moieties [23], respectively. While the Si 2p spectrum are composed of three peaks about Si-C (102.2 eV), Si-N (102.9 eV) and Si-O (103.4 eV) (Fig. 1j) [27]. High-resolution XPS spectra of Y-SiNDs@UA, O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA are shown in Fig. S7-S9 and summarized in Table S1-S3 , along with the corresponding fitted peaks. The existence of multiple covalent bonds (i.e., C=O, C-Si, and Si-O) further confirm that Si atoms had been successfully doped [18,25], and that silicon nanodots had been generated along with rigid network structures. The results confirm that the molecular basis required to generate phosphorescence had been achieved at the structural level.

The spectral characteristics of phosphorescent materials were analyzed by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption, fluorescence, and phosphorescence techniques. Fig. 2 a shows that C-SiNDs@UA exhibits one absorption peak at 268 nm in its UV–vis spectrum, while Y-SiNDs@UA and O-SiNDs@UA exhibit two peaks at 262 and 314 nm, and 297 and 521 nm, respectively, and R-SiNDs@UA exhibits three peaks at 361, 515, and 554 nm. The peaks observed at 268, 262 and 297 nm correspond to π - π* transitions of C=C, while those at 314 and 361 nm are attributed to n - π* transitions of C=O/C=N bond [42,43]. In addition, the peak observed at 521 nm in the spectrum of O-SiNDs@UA and the peaks at 515 and 554 nm observed for R-SiNDs@UA are attributable to characteristic absorption peaks associated with Rh 6G and Rh B [6], respectively.

To elucidate the mechanism responsible for the luminescence of silicon-based RTP materials, we analyzed calcined pure un-doped urea. Fig. S12
a reveals that eUA appears white in daylight, emits blue light when UV-irradiated, and exhibits a blue afterglow when the UV light is extinguished, with fluorescence and phosphorescence maxima observed at 400 and 425 nm ( Fig. S12
b), respectively. Based on the exponential-decay calculations, the fluorescence lifetimes and phosphorescence lifetimes of eUA were calculated to be 3.69 ns and 290 ms ( Fig. S12
c-d), respectively. Subsequently, the fluorescence and phosphorescence quantum yield was measured to be 7.77 % and 10.59 %, respectively; these photophysical properties indicate that eUA can phosphoresce in a short-acting manner. The phosphorescence of eUA is possibly ascribable to the C=C and C=N moieties in its structure. The CA crystals and polymerized triazine ring structures are produced by the deamination and recrystallization of urea during calcination, which simultaneously reduce the nonradiative triplet-exciton deactivation rate to achieve phosphorescence [33,53]. The SiNDs@UA materials show different emission wavelengths because silane plays an important role in regulating this particular emission mode, which is attributable to the new functional groups introduced into the nanodot system upon the addition of silane, resulting in the creation of new surface states [54–56], as reflected in the differences observed for the fluorescence and phosphorescence wavelengths. In addition, the use of the silane functional group as the electron donor increases the efficiency of the electron–hole radiation complex by increasing the electron density, which in turn increases the quantum yield of the material [57].

The intersystem crossing processes that occur in SiNDs@UA were further investigated using femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy. Fig. S13
a shows that C-SiNDs@UA initially exhibits an absorption peak at around 430 nm at 454 fs, which is assignable to the lowest single-linear excited state of C-SiNDs@UA [58], and the intensity of this peak decays gradually over time. While a new absorption peak was observed at approximately 515 nm at 117 ps; this peaks corresponds to the lowest triplet excited state of C-SiNDs@UA [58]. Similar behavior was observed for Y-SiNDs@UA ( Fig. S13
b), in which the absorption peak at 460 nm is related to the lowest singlet excited state, while that observed at 550 nm which appeared at 138 ps, corresponds to the lowest triplet excited state. Taken together with the steady-state phosphorescence results, the changes in the peak positions observed by transient absorption spectroscopy are consistency with intersystem-crossing processes from the singlet-excited to the triplet-excited states in the C-SiNDs@UA and Y-SiNDs@UA systems.

Based on the discussion provided above, we present a mechanistic scheme for the phosphorescence behavior exhibited by the SiNDs@UA materials (Fig. 3 a): (i) Silicon nanodots are formed in situ along with new covalent and hydrogen bonds under the calcination conditions associated with the urea-recrystallization process. Silane, as a precursor, introduces new nanodot surface states, which results in fluorescence and phosphorescence emission changes. (ii) Meanwhile, silane also introduces Si-O and Si-C bonds into the SiNDs, which combine with the rigid network matrix formed by the CA crystals and triazine rings assembled through the recrystallization of urea. The rigid matrix and covalent bonds not only isolate the external quenching agent but also stabilize the internally excited triplet excitons while reducing the non-radiative triplet exciton deactivation rate [32,33,37]. (iii) The abundant hydrogen bonds on the silicon nanodots and CA crystals, which play key roles in restricting vibrations and reducing non-radiative triplet-exciton leaping, are also important reasons for the appearance of phosphorescence [59,60]. FT-IR spectroscopy revealed that all four materials contain abundant functional groups (i.e., N-H and O-H). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy provided further evidence for the conjecture presented above. Oxygen defects in phosphorescent composites are expected to hinder the emission of phosphorescent light [43]. Importantly, the ESR spectra of the four silicon composites reveal the absence of oxygen defects; hence such defects do not affect the phosphorescence emission (Fig. 3e).

The SiNDs@UAs exhibited excellent phosphorescence properties through a mechanism that stabilizes excitons. Hence, we designed two systems with orange and red afterglows using the P-FRET principle; these systems must satisfy the conditions of proximity and spectral overlap. The photographic images show that O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA are uniformly colored, while the TEM images (Fig. 1c-d) also show several uniform spherical structures, indicating that the system is internally fused and that the distances between donors and acceptors are within the range required to trigger P-FRET. Besides, the phosphorescence emissions of C-SiNDs@UA and Y-SiNDs@UA overlap completely with the UV absorption spectra of O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA (Fig. 3d). To distinguish whether the afterglow phenomena of O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA are phosphorescence or delayed fluorescence, the temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectra and transient photoluminescence decay of both O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA were investigated. As shown in Fig. S14
a-b, the afterglow emission peaks remain for both materials and the emission intensity decrease with the temperatures increase from 100 to 300 K, which well coincides with the feature of phosphoresce rather than delayed fluorescence [14,17]. The results of temperature-dependent transient photoluminescence decay also confirm this observation ( Fig. S14
c-d). The lifetimes of both O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA decrease at elevated temperatures, which is attributed to the higher phosphorescent non-radiative deactivation rate exacerbated by increasing temperature [29,40]. Consequently, the afterglow of O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA is phosphorescence rather than delayed fluorescence.

In the optimization of the O-SiNDs@UA system, we record the afterglow spectra of the synthesized materials at different UA and Rh 6G ratios. As shown in Fig. S15
a, the optimal emission spectra shift from 520 nm to around 575 nm with increasing Rh 6G concentration, suggesting that an efficient P-FRET process occurred in the luminescence process [30,61–64]. Meanwhile, the afterglow decay at 520 nm shortens from 562 ms to 312 ms with increasing concentration of the acceptor Rh 6G doping (Fig. 3f), further confirming the existence of the phosphorescence energy transfer from the energy donor to the acceptor. In the fabrication of R-SiNDs@UA with different UA/Rh 6G ratios, with the concentration of Rh B increases, the emission center of the system shifts from 555 nm to 605 nm ( Fig. S15
b), while the afterglow decay at 555 nm shortens from 546 ms to 303 ms (Fig. 3g). These phenomena are all consistent with the characteristics of the P-FRET process. Furthermore, P-FRET was demonstrated by analyzing the femtosecond transient absorption spectra of O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA. Significant negative peaks were observed in the transient absorption spectra during P-FRET. O-SiNDs@UA exhibited a shoulder peak at approximately 524 nm Fig. S13
c, which is a ground-state bleaching (GSB) feature because ground-state molecules are pumped into their excited states owing to receptor Rh 6G, while the signal near 575 nm corresponds to the stimulated emission (SE) from Rh 6G. The transient absorption spectrum of R-SiNDs@UA (
Fig. S13
d) shows similar features, in which the shoulder peak at 540 nm corresponds to the absorption of Rh B, with the signal at 600 nm corresponding to the stimulated emission from Rh B [61].

To further investigate the luminescence process and internal structure of O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA, we first verified the presence of rhodamine residues in the afterglow material by FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. S16
a, the distinct peaks around 1455, 1593, 1716, and 2929 cm−1 correspond to C-N, C=C, C=O, and C-H vibrations [65,66], respectively. These characteristic peaks are present in both Rh 6G and O-SiNDs@UA, which further supports the presence of Rh 6G-associated functional groups in O-SiNDs@UA. The results of the FT-IR spectra of Rh B and R-SiNDs@UA also show that characteristic peaks (C-N, C=C, C=O, C-H, etc.) exist in R-SiNDs@UA ( Fig. S16
b). In addition, the characteristic peaks of rhodamine residues are also present in the UV absorption spectra (Fig. 2a). Therefore, these results indicate the presence of residual functional groups of rhodamines on the surface of the SiNDs after hydrothermal synthesis, which would provide a new surface state in the photoluminescence process [62,67]. Furthermore, we tested the afterglow in the mixture of Y-SiNDs@UA and Rh B to corroborate the conditions required for P-FRET. Organic dyes are subject to quenching in their natural powder state due to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect, and the isolation of the dyes within a framework matrix is an important method of preparing luminescent materials [68,69]. When Y-SiNDs@UA and Rh B are mixed, there is no luminescence due to the ACQ effect of Rh B, only the phosphorescence of Y-SiNDs@UA is visible ( Fig. S16
c). Therefore the appropriate reaction is required to integrate Rh B into the matrix, not only to avoid interference with ACQ effects, but also to shorten the distance between the energy donor and acceptor to achieve P-FRET.

To further determine the internal composition of O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA, we synthesized two materials with orange and red afterglow by a two-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, several characterizations of Rh 6G/C-SiNDs@UA and Rh B/Y-SiNDs@UA were carried out and compared with the results of O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA. As shown in Fig. S17
a-b, the Rh 6G/C-SiNDs@UA and Rh B/Y-SiNDs@UA display a quasi-spherical shape with an average diameter of about 2.5 and 2.4 nm, respectively, which are similar to the appearance of O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA. The FT-IR spectra of the four materials ( Fig. S17
c) show that the SiNDs@UAs have very similar characteristic peaks. The XPS spectra of the four materials display the same peaks corresponding to carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and silicon ( Fig. S17
d). The results confirm that the molecular basis required to generate P-FRET afterglow had been achieved at the same structural level. The correlation of afterglow materials are also explored by spectroscopic properties. As shown in Fig. S18
a-b, both Rh 6G/C-SiNDs@UA and O-SiNDs@UA have optical emission peaks positioned at 575 nm and afterglow lifetimes of 491 and 495 ms respectively, indicating that both materials have similar afterglow emission centers. Meanwhile, the emission peaks of both Rh B/Y-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA are at 605 nm and the afterglow lifetimes are 486 and 481 ms respectively ( Fig. S18
c-d), also indicating that the emission centers of the two materials are similar. In the afterglow luminescence process of Rh 6G/C-SiNDs@UA and Rh B/Y-SiNDs@UA, the P-FRET process is achieved by immobilizing the rhodamine residual functional groups on the surface of the SiNDs as energy acceptor in the dissolved and recrystallized matrix, which is the common way in the preparation of afterglow materials using rhodamine, and the common approach of these works is to confine the energy donors with phosphorescence and the dye molecules as acceptors together in a stable matrix (PVA. SiO2, etc.) by a hydrothermal reaction [6,64,67,70]. According to the analysis results obtained in comparison with the two-step method, O-SiNDs@UA and Rh 6G/C-SiNDs@UA are very similar in their structural composition, as well as R-SiNDs@UA and Rh B/Y-SiNDs@UA are also similar in the structural composition. Finally, we assumed that energy transfer from triplet states of a donor (C-SiNDs@UA) to singlet states of a fluorescent acceptor (Rh 6G residues) in the O-SiNDs@UA system would be a reliable strategy to explain the afterglow process, as well as the energy donor (Y-SiNDs@UA) to energy acceptor (Rh B residues) in the R-SiNDs@UA system. Nevertheless, compared to the two-step method, the one-step method is more convenient and simple, which can also reduce the uncontrollable risks associated with multi-step operations, so the one-step method was chosen for the preparation of afterglow materials in this work.

Consequently, the P-FRET mechanism for the R-SiNDs@UA system can be rationalized by combining the above analyses (Fig. 3b): Firstly, some of the photoexcited singlet excitons (S1 E) in the donor matrix of the excited state are transferred directly to the singlet excited state of the acceptor (S1 M) by a S1 E–S1 M FRET process, after which S1 M returns to the ground state via radiative decay to realize fluorescence emission. Meanwhile, under the given specific excitation conditions, photoexcited S1 E can transfer the excited triplet excitons of the donor (T1 E) through an alternative process involving intersystem crossing, after which they are continuously transferred to S1 M via the T1 E–S1 M P-FRET pathway. Because the excited triplet state is slowly released when the excitation light source was extinguished, the S1 M excitons also revert to the ground state slowly by radiative decay to produce afterglow emissions [30]. The luminescence schematic for the O-SiNDs@UA and R-SiNDs@UA material are shown in Fig. S19 and Fig. 3c based on the reasoning presented above, respectively.

Luminescent materials have been used to develop a wide range of anti-counterfeiting patterns based on their adjustable fluorescence emissions and their abilities to respond to a variety of excitation bands. However, because their colors are easily adjusted, their fluorescence emissions are also easily imitated and are not sufficiently unique. A more effective method involves the use of multi-color afterglow for a specific duration following removal of the excitation source, which eliminates background interference and improves anti-counterfeiting performance. By combining and matching multiple colors and different afterglow times, single-encryption, multiple-encryption, and 5D encryption modes involving an optical filter can be realized. In addition, the multicolor afterglow achieved in this study is realized at the same excitation wavelength (λex = 365 nm), which greatly improves the convenience of the information encryption process and facilitates various color combinations without requiring different excitation sources.

Fig. 4 a shows how the four color materials are used to design a butterfly pattern. The four colors and the butterfly parts they represent are clearly observed when the UV light source is extinguished; hence their information-carrying and transmission roles are realized. Silicon nanodots with no phosphorescence but only emitting with blue fluorescence were synthesized in a previous study [71], and C-SiNDs@UA emits similar blue light that is difficult to distinguish with the naked eye when UV-irradiated (Fig. 4b). However, the phosphorescent material shows the letter “USTC” after the UV lamp is turned off, which demonstrates single-encryption use. Multiple colors also facilitate the development of multiple-message encryption methods. We used Morse code as a cipher to represent various alphabetic messages through various combinations of dots and lines. Fig. 4c shows double encryption using different colors. The patterns were designed using three materials: C-SiNDs@UA, Y-SiNDs@UA, and O-SiNDs@UA. Cyan represents common information, yellow represents the unique color of Code 1, and orange represents the unique color of Code 2. The correct information for Code 1 (CHEMISTRY) was obtained after reading the cyan and yellow combination. In a similar manner, the cyan and orange combination provides information for Code 2 (CHOCOLATE). The differently gradated information encryptions demonstrate the excellent encoding capabilities of the SiNDs@UA materials developed in this study.

Observing the afterglow patterns of the materials reveals that Y-SiNDs@UA and O-SiNDs@UA exhibit afterglows with observable intensities even at wavelengths greater than 600 nm. Multiple information encryption with the same color but different time lengths can be cleverly achieved using an optical filter (λcut-off = 600 nm) in combination with the 600 nm emission from R-SiNDs@UA. The anti-counterfeiting model shown in Fig. 5 a displays a lack of unique features under daylight or UV light; however, mixed colors are observed after the UV light was turned off, which was recognized as “false” by the computer. However, a uniformly red afterglow was observed when the optical filter was reapplied, which is recognized as “true” at this time. These properties facilitate the development of the 5D anti-counterfeiting strategy. 5D anti-counterfeiting is a novel application of multi-color afterglow materials, which is more advanced than ordinary anti-counterfeiting modes. 5D represents five dimensions of information coding, with one coded information corresponding to one dimension [72,73]. Fig. 5b shows information encoded using Y-SiNDs@UA, O-SiNDs@UA, and R-SiNDs@UA. Only the wrong 5D codes 1 and code 2 were obtained when the UV lamp is on or off when unaided by the filter. However, the correct 5D code 3 “BLUEBERRY” is read first using the filter when the UV light is extinguished. Because the afterglow durations of these three materials are different in the red region of the spectrum, the low-intensity red color of Y-SiNDs@UA disappears first (after 2 s), with the 5D code 4 “BANANA” displayed at this time. Similarly, since the red color of O-SiNDs@UA is less intense than that of R-SiNDs@UA, its color disappears after 4 s, and the 5D code 5 “DATE” displayed. This anti-counterfeiting strategy can be applied to higher-level encryption scenarios, in which only interference information is obtained in UV-irradiation and afterglow modes, and the assistance of a filter is required to accurately read the encrypted content, thereby enabling better concealment of the correct information.

In summary, we successfully demonstrate a reliable and innovative strategy for the synthesis of multi-color afterglow materials from silane as a precursor, while ingeniously achieving four distinct afterglow colors using the same excitation source (λex = 365 nm). Notably, silane, as a precursor, introduces the Si-C and Si-O bonds into the SiNDs; these bonds play important roles in reducing the non-radiative relaxation of the system. Furthermore, the SiNDs are connected and immobilized by a rigid network structure that include CA crystals and triazine ring structures, through covalent and hydrogen bonds. This microenvironment, which consists of a rigid structure, plays a critical role in stabilizing the triplet SiNDs excitions. Intriguingly, SiNDs act as phosphorescent donors while being immobilized, and demonstrate excellent phosphorescence properties and the P-FRET principle. Orange and red afterglow materials were obtained by adding Rh 6G and Rh B to the precursors of cyan and yellow phosphorescent systems, respectively. These unique features enabled the as-prepared high-performance afterglow materials to be successfully used in single and multiple information security, and we also established a red-afterglow-based 5D information encryption system in a SiNDs-related field. More importantly, based on the abundance of silicon on Earth, the simple and standardized manufacturing process developed in this study can easily be extended to other normal silanes, which provides further significant potential for future large-scale development. Finally, this work demonstrated the feasibility of using silane to synthesize multi-color phosphorescent materials and provides novel design principles and insights for the construction of silicon-based afterglow materials for advanced information encryption and innovative applications.
